title: Exercise Salaknib 2026 Phase 1: Japan and Australia Join as Full Participants for First Time in 'Largest-Ever' Land Forces Drill
detail: Exercise Salaknib 2026 Phase 1 — the annual US-Philippines bilateral land forces exercise — is ongoing at Fort Magsaysay and Camp Tecson (April 6–17) with more than 7,000 troops from the United States, Philippines, Japan, and Australia, making it the largest iteration in the exercise's history. Japan Ground Self-Defense Force and Australian Army forces are participating as full-fledged partners for the first time (previously as observers), reflecting the rapid deepening of multilateral ground force interoperability in the Indo-Pacific ahead of Balikatan 2026 (April 20–May 8). Exercises include jungle warfare, aviation operations, live-fire anti-armor training using Carl Gustaf recoilless rifles, electronic warfare, combined arms maneuvers, and a 17-day Jungle Operations Training Course at Camp Tecson. The exercise includes electronic warfare training that reflects lessons from Ukraine and the Iran-Israel conflict, emphasizing electromagnetic spectrum dominance. US Army Pacific described Salaknib 2026 as demonstrating 'how like-minded democracies are working together to address shared security challenges in the region.' Phase 2 follows in May and June 2026 after Balikatan 2026 concludes.

media.caption: U.S. and Philippine soldiers conduct electronic warfare training at Exercise Salaknib 2026, Fort Magsaysay, Philippines, April 2026

title: AFP Clarifies: Philippines Government Will Own Planned US Fuel Depot Facilities Under EDCA Framework
detail: The Armed Forces of the Philippines (AFP) clarified on April 11, 2026 that the planned US Defense Fuel Support Point (DFSP) facilities in the Davao region will remain under Philippine government ownership and control — consistent with the Enhanced Defense Cooperation Agreement (EDCA) framework, under which all US-constructed facilities at EDCA sites are Philippine-owned assets. Philippine Navy Rear Admiral Roy Vincent Trinidad, West Philippine Sea spokesperson, stated the fuel depot 'will not be a magnet for attacks but will actually have benefits' for the Philippines' maritime security posture, citing its role in sustaining deployed forces in the West Philippine Sea and southern border areas. The AFP's clarification came after opposition voices questioned whether the arrangement constituted a de facto US military basing extension under the Philippine Constitution. Officials noted the details have 'yet to be finalized' but that the EDCA framework clearly establishes Philippine sovereignty over any constructed facility.

media.caption: AFP clarifies Philippines government ownership of planned US fuel depot facilities under EDCA
